content.text: Action Item Ownership and Closure SLAs

Postmortems generate action items, but action items without owners and deadlines quietly rot. A clear ownership model with severity-tiered SLAs is the difference between a postmortem that drives change and one that produces a document no one revisits. This sub-subtopic covers how to assign ownership, set and enforce closure SLAs, and build the operational cadence that keeps follow-through visible.

The blameless postmortem surfaces what failed. The action items define what changes to prevent recurrence. If those items drift into backlog limbo, the organisation learns nothing operationally — it only learns how to write postmortems. The failure mode is predictable: a P0 outage occurs, a thorough postmortem is published, fifteen action items are created, and six months later the same class of failure recurs because nine of those items were never completed.
A single named human owner per item
A due date calibrated to incident severity
A status that is visible without needing to ask the owner
A process that escalates when SLAs are approaching or breached

attrs.alt: Action Item Ownership and Closure SLA Framework

content.text: Closure SLAs are anchored to incident severity because the risk of non-completion scales with the original blast radius. A SEV-1 that took down customer-facing AI inference for 40 minutes carries far higher recurrence risk than a SEV-3 data-quality blip that affected one internal report.
The SLA clock starts from the
, not the incident resolution date. This gives the team time to draft the postmortem, identify root causes, and produce well-scoped action items before the timer starts.
Every action item must have exactly one named owner. Assigning to a team means assigning to no one. The owner is the person who will close the item or escalate when they cannot.
If an item requires multiple people (e.g., platform changes plus application changes), split it into two separate action items, each with a distinct owner.
Owner Accountability Does Not Mean Owner Implementation

The owner drives closure — they can and should delegate implementation work. What they cannot delegate is the responsibility for status updates, escalation, and verification that the item is genuinely resolved.

This distinction is critical for cross-team items. If the postmortem reveals that the ML platform team needs to add circuit-breaker logic, the named owner may be an ML engineer who does not write platform code. Their job is to coordinate with the platform team, track progress, and confirm when the fix is deployed.
: on-call eng who handled the incident, unless the fix is clearly in another team's domain
: the team that owns the affected system, not the team that discovered the problem
: engineering manager or tech lead, since these require organisational authority
: SRE/platform team if it's infra; application team if it's missing metrics in their service
An action item without a definition of done will be closed prematurely or never closed at all. Every item should state the evidence that proves it is complete.
The fix is deployed to production
Monitoring confirms the fix is effective
(metric moved in expected direction or alert no longer fires)
The postmortem is updated with a link to the change and confirmation of verification
For process or documentation items, replace the first two criteria with the appropriate evidence (e.g., runbook published at URL X, incident response drill completed on date Y).
"Add 30s timeout on inference endpoint; alert fires if p99 > 25s; verified via load test on staging + 48h prod metrics"
"Add token_budget_exhausted alert at 80% threshold; silence rate < 5% for 2 weeks post-deploy"
"Publish runbook at docs.internal/ai-ops/oom-recovery; include rollback steps; reviewed by on-call rotation"
For SEV-1 action items, the on-call rotation lead runs a short daily check-in (5 minutes maximum) on outstanding items:

SEV-1 Action Item Daily Check (async Slack thread)

Owner: [name] | Item: [short title]
Status: IN_PROGRESS | AT_RISK | BLOCKED
Progress since yesterday: [one line]
ETA to close: [date]
Blocker (if any): [team / dependency]
If an item is AT_RISK (fewer than 48 hours to SLA breach), the incident commander from the original incident is notified and pulls the owner into a focused sync.

The postmortem owner chairs a 15-minute twice-weekly review of all open SEV-2 items from the last 30 days. Items transition from IN_PROGRESS to AT_RISK to BREACHED on a shared board visible to the engineering team.

SEV-3 items live in the team's sprint backlog. The rule is simple: they must appear in at least one sprint every 30 days. An item that has not been picked up in 30 days is flagged for deferral or escalation.
Tracking Infrastructure for AI Platform Teams
For most AI platform teams, a combination of your issue tracker and a dedicated Slack channel is sufficient:

# Jira action item template
summary: "[POST-{INC_ID}] {short description}"
labels:
  - postmortem-action
  - sev-{severity}
custom_fields:
  postmortem_link: "https://notion.so/postmortems/{id}"
  closure_criteria: |
    1. ...
    2. ...
  evidence_of_completion: ""   # filled at closure
  sla_due_date: "{date}"

# Slack integration: post daily digest of AT_RISK items to #eng-incidents
Do not rely on humans to remember SLA deadlines. Build or configure automated reminders:
SLA breaches should be visible, not shameful. The goal is to surface systemic blockers, not to punish individuals.
Item elevated to next sprint as P0 if blocked by another team's prioritisation
VP Engineering notified if item is SEV-1 and remains open after 5 business days

Not every action item needs to be closed on schedule. Some are genuinely blocked by external dependencies, sequencing requirements, or changed priorities. A deferred item is not a failed item — a silently stale item is.
Document the reason in the postmortem and the issue tracker
Set a new target date in the next sprint
Get acknowledgement from the engineering manager

The anti-pattern is closing an action item as 'Done' when the underlying fix was not verified. Premature closure is worse than no closure — it creates false confidence that the system has been hardened.
Teams that measure action item health close items faster and more reliably than those that do not. Track:
Avg days from publication to closure, by severity
P50/P90 age of currently open items
% of items deferred at least once

Review these metrics in your quarterly engineering review alongside SLA metrics and error budget consumption. Persistent SLA breaches are an organisational signal: either postmortems are generating too many items, items are too large, or teams are under-resourced.
AI platform incidents frequently produce action items that are structurally different from pure software bugs:
(e.g., 'retrain model with corrected labels within 7 days') — require ML platform scheduling, not just a code change
(e.g., 'add eval coverage for adversarial prompt injection') — cross-team, involves ML + security
(e.g., 'implement statistical drift monitor on embedding distributions') — research-adjacent, may take longer than standard SLAs

For model-related items that require retraining or significant evaluation work, use a modified SLA: define a milestone-based closure criteria (e.g., 'data collected by day 3, training run by day 7, evaluation passed by day 14') rather than a single deadline.
If the incident involved a safety-relevant failure (e.g., model produced harmful output at scale, safety filter bypassed, RLHF reward signal corrupted), action items should be:
with a 'safety' label that blocks the affected system from serving new traffic until closed
by a senior engineer with the authority to gate deployment
by the safety/policy team in addition to engineering before closure

Safety-tagged action items carry an implicit SLA of: fix before the next deployment to production, regardless of severity. A SEV-3 that involved a safety failure has a shorter effective SLA than the standard 30 days.
Common Failure Patterns and How to Fix Them
Even teams with well-intentioned postmortem processes fall into predictable traps around action item follow-through:

A thorough postmortem generates 20 action items. Ten are completed in the first week because they are small and clearly scoped. The remaining ten require cross-team coordination, platform changes, or architectural rethinking. They stall indefinitely.

Fix: Cap action items at 5–7 per postmortem for SEV-2 and below. Consolidate systemic themes into one item per theme. If there are genuinely 20 distinct things to fix, the postmortem revealed a systemic architecture problem — open a separate design initiative, not 20 individual tickets.

The same root cause (e.g., missing circuit breakers on LLM API calls) manifests across three incidents in a quarter. Each postmortem generates an action item to 'add circuit breakers'. None of the three items is ever linked, so each team sees only their own item and assumes someone else fixed the underlying problem.

Fix: During postmortem creation, search the open action item backlog for similar items before creating a new one. If a duplicate exists, close the new item and add a comment to the original. Assign a single owner who coordinates the fix across all affected systems.

An action item reads: 'Improve model fallback behavior'. The owner marks it done after adding a 500ms timeout. Two weeks later a different failure mode exposes that the fallback does not handle context-length errors — which was the original incident's root cause.

Fix: Every action item must reference the specific failure mode from the postmortem. The closure criteria must include a test case (automated or manual) that exercises that exact failure mode. If you cannot write a test for the fix, you probably do not understand the fix.

An action item is assigned to Engineer A. Two weeks later Engineer A moves to a different team. The item is reassigned to whoever is on-call at the time. That person has no context and the item drifts.

Fix: When an owner changes teams mid-item, the engineering manager explicitly re-assigns it in the sprint planning meeting. The outgoing owner writes a handoff note (3–5 sentences) directly in the issue tracker comment, summarising current state, remaining work, and any open questions.
For high-severity incidents, action items can be linked to deployment gates — CI/CD checks that block production deployments until critical fixes are verified.

# GitHub Actions: deployment gate for open SEV-1 postmortem items
name: Deployment Safety Gate
on:
  pull_request:
    branches: [main]

jobs:
  check-open-sev1-items:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - name: Check for blocking postmortem items
        run: |
          OPEN_ITEMS=$(gh issue list \
            --label "postmortem-action,sev-1,deployment-blocker" \
            --state open \
            --json number,title \
            --jq 'length')

          if [ "$OPEN_ITEMS" -gt 0 ]; then
            echo "BLOCKED: $OPEN_ITEMS open SEV-1 postmortem action items"
            gh issue list \
              --label "postmortem-action,sev-1,deployment-blocker" \
              --state open
            exit 1
          fi
          echo "No blocking postmortem items - deployment allowed"
        env:
          GH_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}

Use this pattern sparingly — only for SEV-1 items tagged as 'deployment-blocker'. Blocking all deployments on any open action item will immediately create incentive to skip the tagging, defeating the purpose.
In addition to per-incident tracking, run a quarterly retrospective specifically on action item health. Thirty minutes per quarter is sufficient. The agenda:
Review SLA compliance rate for the quarter
— if it is more than 90 days old, it needs a decision: prioritise now or formally defer to next quarter
— if 'observability' appears in 6 postmortems this quarter, that is a platform investment signal
— publicly acknowledge engineers who drove complex fixes to completion

The quarterly retrospective serves a second purpose: it creates a forcing function for deferred items that have been quietly accumulating. Teams that skip this review tend to accumulate technical debt in exactly the areas where they have had the most failures.

Postmortem action items close the loop between incident response and system improvement. The ownership model is simple: one named human, one due date, explicit closure criteria, and a status that is visible without asking. Severity-tiered SLAs (SEV-1: 3 days, SEV-2: 7 days, SEV-3: 30 days) ensure that the highest-risk items receive the most urgent follow-through. Automated SLA alerting, twice-weekly review cadences for SEV-1/2, and quarterly metrics on closure rates turn intent into operational discipline. For AI platforms, model-related and safety-critical items warrant modified closure criteria that reflect their longer timelines and higher stakes.
One owner per item — teams are not owners
SLA clock starts at postmortem publication, not incident resolution
Closure requires deployed fix + monitoring confirmation + postmortem updated
Breached SLAs escalate up the management chain, not the blame chain
Safety-tagged items block deployment until closed, regardless of severity
